content.text: is the modern, standardized configuration file for Python packaging that defines how to build, install, and manage your project. It's the "birth certificate" for your code, replacing the legacy
What it is — Clear definition and context
(not executable Python code) that tells Python exactly how to build, install, and manage your project. It became the standard through PEP 517, 518, and 621.
Single file for build system, metadata, dependencies, and tools
Why it matters — Purpose and importance
solves critical problems from the legacy
: Executable Python code runs on install
: Works with Poetry, Hatch, Flit, setuptools
: Modern standard for all new projects
: Everything (metadata, dependencies, build config) in one file
: No executable code runs during installation
: Clean, structured TOML format vs Python code
: Works with multiple build backends (Poetry, Hatch, setuptools)
: Metadata appears on Python Package Index when published
Requires learning TOML syntax (different from Python)
Can't use Python logic for conditional configuration (unlike
Requires modern pip (≥21.3) and build tools

How it works — The PEP 517/518 build protocol
first, creates an isolated virtual environment, installs the declared
packages into it, then calls the
module's hooks. This isolation means the build environment is clean and reproducible regardless of what is installed in the user's environment — a major improvement over
which ran in the calling environment.

[project]
dependencies = [
    # Minimum version — install gets any compatible release
    "requests>=2.28.0",
    # Locked to minor: allows patches but not major/minor bumps
    "transformers==4.40.*",
    # Strict compatibility range for a library with known breaking change
    "pandas>=2.0.0, <3.0.0",
    # Environment markers: only install on Windows
    "pywin32>=300; sys_platform == 'win32'",
    # Python version conditional
    "tomllib>=2.0.0; python_version < '3.11'",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
# pip install mypackage[dev]
dev = [
    "pytest>=7.0.0",
    "pytest-cov>=4.0.0",
    "black>=23.0.0",
    "ruff>=0.4.0",
    "mypy>=1.0.0",
]
# pip install mypackage[gpu]
gpu = [
    "torch>=2.1.0",
    "nvidia-cuda-runtime-cu12>=12.0.0",
]
A complete CI/CD pipeline for a
-based package involves four stages: install dependencies, run tests, build distributions, and publish to PyPI. GitHub Actions makes this straightforward.

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Test and Release

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ["v*"]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.10", "3.11", "3.12"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}

      - name: Install with dev extras
        run: pip install -e ".[dev]"

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest --cov=my_package --cov-report=xml

      - name: Type check
        run: mypy my_package/

  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - run: pip install build
      - run: python -m build
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/

  publish:
    # Only publish on version tags
    if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: pypi
    permissions:
      id-token: write   # Required for Trusted Publishing (no API token needed)
    steps:
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/
      - uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1

+ OIDC) removes the need to store a PyPI API token as a GitHub secret. PyPI verifies that the publish request comes from your specific repository and workflow via OIDC, which is more secure than static tokens.

# Option 1: Static version in pyproject.toml
[project]
version = "1.2.3"

# Option 2: Dynamic version from git tags (setuptools-scm)
[project]
dynamic = ["version"]

[tool.setuptools_scm]
# version = "1.2.3.post1+gabcdef1" derived from git tags
# tag_regex = "v(?P<version>[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+)"

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=61.0", "setuptools-scm>=8.0"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"
, the package version is derived automatically from the most recent git tag. Running
triggers the release pipeline without any manual version file edits.

Package installation speed depends on whether a pre-built wheel is available. Installing from a wheel is nearly instantaneous (just extraction), while building from source requires running the backend. Here is a comparison of install times for a medium-sized ML package:
(from Astral) is a Rust-based pip/venv replacement that is 10–100× faster than pip for dependency resolution. It reads
for reproducible installs. For CI pipelines that install dependencies on every run, switching to
can reduce CI time by 2–3 minutes.

# Install uv
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Create venv and install in one step (10x faster than pip venv + pip install)
uv venv
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

# Lock dependencies for reproducible CI
uv pip compile pyproject.toml --output-file requirements.lock
uv pip sync requirements.lock

# Or use uv's own project management (replaces Poetry)
uv init my-project   # creates pyproject.toml
uv add requests      # adds to [project.dependencies]
uv add --dev pytest  # adds to [project.optional-dependencies.dev]

The pip dependency resolver (backtracking resolver, introduced in pip 20.3) can be slow for packages with many transitive dependencies. It backtracks when constraints conflict, which can take O(2^N) in worst case.
uses a SAT solver approach that avoids backtracking and resolves in near-linear time.
Python package supply-chain attacks have increased significantly: typosquatting (publishing
), dependency confusion attacks, and compromised maintainer accounts.
alone does not defend against these, but the ecosystem provides several layers of protection.

# Generate hashes for all dependencies
pip-compile pyproject.toml --generate-hashes --output-file requirements.lock

# Install with hash verification (fails if any file is tampered)
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
annotation. If any wheel or sdist is replaced (even by the package author), the hash check fails and the install aborts. This prevents
where an attacker replaces a package between your lock file generation and your production deploy.

# In your GitHub Actions release workflow:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      hashes: ${{ steps.hash.outputs.hashes }}
    steps:
      - name: Build distributions
        run: python -m build
      - name: Compute hashes
        id: hash
        run: |
          echo "hashes=$(sha256sum dist/* | base64 -w0)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  # Generate SLSA provenance attestation
  provenance:
    needs: build
    uses: slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_generic_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0
    with:
      base64-subjects: "${{ needs.build.outputs.hashes }}"
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: write

provenance gives users cryptographic proof that your package was built from a specific git commit in a specific GitHub Actions run. Consumers can verify that the wheel on PyPI was built by your CI system and has not been tampered with.
